Where Craft
Meets the Web

Designing a full multi-page website for a fictional woodworking studio — capturing the warmth, texture, and heritage of handmade craft in a digital format, without losing the soul of the work in the process.

Personal Project
Responsive Website
Home, About, Portfolio, Contact
Figma
01
Project Overview

Bringing a Workshop
to the Web

Nature's Edge Carpentry is a personal project conceived as a design challenge: how do you translate something as tactile, physical, and handmade as woodworking into a digital experience that doesn't feel sterile or disconnected from the craft? The website needed to feel like it was made by hand — warm, considered, unhurried — while still functioning as a professional business platform capable of showcasing work, telling the brand story, and converting visitors into clients.

The project spans four complete pages — Homepage, About, Portfolio, and Contact — each with its own layout logic and purpose, all unified by a single visual language built around the textures, tones, and materials of the workshop itself.

4+
Pages designed

4

1
Cohesive identity

Deliverables

Homepage About Page Portfolio Page Contact Page Navigation System Brand Pillars Review Section Team Section Contact Form
02
The Design Brief

Designing for
Craft, Not Clicks

Trade and craft businesses occupy a difficult space online. Their work is deeply physical — the grain of the wood, the weight of a hand-planed surface, the smell of a workshop — and digital media struggles to communicate any of that. Most carpentry websites default to one of two failed approaches: a generic business template with stock photos, or a portfolio dump with no narrative or brand identity.

The brief for Nature's Edge Carpentry was to design something in between: a website with the professionalism of a premium agency site and the warmth and personality of a family-run workshop. The fictional brand had a clear identity — nature-inspired, sustainability-committed, precision-obsessed — and the design needed to make visitors feel those values before they read a single word about them.

The Four Brand Pillars

🌲

Nature Inspired

Incorporating the essence of wood and its surroundings into every project.

🔨

Craftsmanship

Skilled artisans bringing decades of expertise and meticulous attention to every piece.

♻️

Sustainability

Responsibly sourced materials and a deep commitment to protecting the environment.

🎯

Precision

From design to installation, an unwavering commitment to excellence sets the work apart.

🪵

Core design question: Can a website feel handmade? Not rough or unpolished — but warm, deliberate, and tactile in the way that a piece of furniture is when you run your hand across it. Every colour, font, and layout choice was made with that question in mind.

03
Page-by-Page Breakdown

Four Pages,
One Story

Each page in the Nature's Edge system serves a distinct function in the visitor journey — from discovery to trust-building to conversion. The design had to ensure that every page could stand alone while feeling like part of a deliberate whole.

A
Homepage

First Impressions in Wood & Light

The homepage leads with a full-bleed hero — a craftsman at work, hands visible, tools present — that immediately establishes authenticity. The headline is centred and confident: "Welcome to Nature's Edge Carpentry." No teaser copy, no tagline subversion — just a direct, honest introduction that trusts the imagery to do the emotional work.

  • Four brand pillar icons on a warm terracotta band communicate values in seconds
  • Full-bleed woodworking tools quote section — "Woodworking, it is our specialty and our primary focus." — adds brand voice mid-scroll
  • Testimonial carousel with botanical leaf background creates a calm, organic close to the page
  • Dark brown footer grounds the page and reinforces the brand's earthy, premium identity
B
About Page

The Story Behind the Sawdust

The About page uses an alternating left-right text-and-image layout that creates a sense of conversation and movement through the page. Rather than placing all content in one column, the design treats each section — Our Story, Our Mission, Our Vision — as its own compositional moment.

  • Our Story opens with a dark header panel — the brand's brown — that signals gravitas and history
  • Workshop photography and hands-on imagery keeps the page grounded in the physical reality of the craft
  • Team section with circular portrait frames adds human faces to what could otherwise feel abstract
  • Four brand pillar strip at the bottom echoes the homepage — creating a consistent system signature across pages
C
Portfolio Page

Work Organised by the Way Clients Think

The portfolio page divides work into three categories: Furnitures, Home Decor, and Outdoor Creations. This taxonomy mirrors how prospective clients actually think about what they need — not by technique or material, but by where in their life or home the piece will live.

  • Each category leads with a large 2×2 image grid giving equal visual weight to all pieces
  • "See Projects →" links at category level invite deeper exploration without overwhelming the scroll
  • Clean white background within the page creates maximum contrast for the photography to breathe
  • Category headings in the brand's rust/terracotta colour create a visual rhythm as users scroll
D
Contact Page

A Form That Earns the Enquiry

The contact page opens with a dark brown header panel containing the business address, phone number, and email — all the information a potential client needs to feel that the business is real, local, and reachable. Only after establishing this credibility does the form appear.

  • Address, phone, and email surfaced immediately — not buried in a sidebar or below the form
  • Form fields use the brand's rust colour as border accents — keeping the form on-brand without being distracting
  • "Interested in working together? Let's chat!" sub-heading uses approachable, human language before the formal fields
  • Minimal form design — name, email, subject, message — respects the user's time and removes friction
04
Key Design Decisions

The Choices That
Made It Feel
Hand-Built

Deep Brown as the Brand Anchor

The primary colour — a deep, almost mahogany brown (#3D1208) — is the single most important design decision in the entire project. It immediately communicates the material the brand works with. Brown is wood. It's also warmth, age, expertise, and heritage. Used for the nav, hero overlays, section accents, and footer, it creates a consistent visual throughline that makes every page feel connected without repeating the same layout.

Terracotta as the Secondary

The warm terracotta (#B85C38) used for the brand pillar band, category headings, and section labels provides the visual energy the deep brown can't. Where brown says "heritage and stability," terracotta says "warmth and craft." Together they create a palette that feels like the materials themselves — dark timber and warm wood shavings, shadow and highlight, the workshop at golden hour.

Centred Hero Typography

The homepage hero uses centred, white typography over a full-bleed photograph of hands at work. This is a deliberate departure from the split-column hero common in modern web design. Centred type on photography is a classic editorial technique — it puts the craft first and the words second, which is exactly the right hierarchy for a business whose product is the work itself.

Portfolio Taxonomy by Use Case

Organising the portfolio into Furnitures, Home Decor, and Outdoor Creations rather than by technique or material was a user-centred decision. A client coming to commission a dining table doesn't think "I want joinery." They think "I want furniture for my dining room." Designing around how users think, not how craftspeople categorise their work, makes the portfolio immediately navigable for the people who matter most.

The Botanical Review Background

The testimonial section uses a dark botanical leaf pattern as its background — lush, layered, organic. This is a deliberate reference to the "Nature" in Nature's Edge. It also provides a complete contrast to the wood and tools photography used elsewhere on the page, creating a moment of calm and greenery that makes the section feel like a pause — appropriately placed where potential clients read what others have said before deciding to get in touch.

"A website for a woodworker should feel like wood. Not wood-coloured — wood-hearted: warm, patient, and made with care."

05
Colour & Visual Language

A Palette Pulled
from the Workshop

Every colour in the Nature's Edge palette can be found in the physical materials and environment of a woodworking workshop — no abstractions, no trend-chasing. The palette is the brand.

Mahogany
#3D1208

Brand primary. Nav, footer, headers. The colour of dark timber — heritage, depth, authority.

Terracotta
#B85C38

Accents, headings, labels. Warm wood shavings, workshop dust, golden afternoon light.

Linen White
#FAF7F4

Page backgrounds. Not clinical white — warm and slightly toasted, like raw linen or wood dust in sunlight.

Raw Sand
#E8D5C4

Section backgrounds and dividers. The colour of unfinished pine — soft, natural, grounding.

06
Takeaways & Reflections

What Designing
for Craft Teaches You

Most UI work happens in categories where digital is native — apps, dashboards, e-commerce. Designing for a trade craft like carpentry is a different creative challenge entirely. The work you're representing resists digitisation by its very nature. This project taught me to think about UI design not just as interface design, but as material design — asking what material, what texture, what weight the digital surface should evoke.

🪵

Material should inform palette

Every colour came from wood. This constraint made the palette feel inevitable rather than designed — and that authenticity is exactly what a craft brand needs.

📐

Four pages forces system thinking

With a homepage you make decisions. With four pages you build a system. The pillar strip appearing on both the homepage and about page wasn't a copy-paste — it was a system at work.

🗂️

Taxonomy is UX, not admin

Organising the portfolio by room type rather than technique was the single biggest user experience decision in the project. How you name and structure categories shapes how visitors navigate and what they find.

🤲

Show hands, not products

Photography of hands at work builds more trust for a craft business than product shots alone. The craft is the person — showing the maker is showing the value proposition.

📋

Contact pages need credibility first

Address and phone number before the form. For a local trade business, establishing physical presence is a trust signal. The form is the conversion — the contact info earns the right to ask for it.

🌿

Nature is a design motif, not decoration

The botanical review section background wasn't decorative — it was thematic. When the brand name includes "Nature," that word must appear visually, not just linguistically, across the design.

"The best websites for craft businesses don't try to be digital. They try to be honest representations of something made by hand."

Nature's Edge
